title: Ruptured Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
oneLiner: A sudden, catastrophic breach of a weakened aortic wall in elderly smokers/males—presenting with shock, abdominal/back pain, and a pulsatile mass; time-to-OR defines survival.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 72-year-old male smoker with known hypertension arrives via ambulance. He was lifting a heavy box at home when he felt a sudden “tearing” sensation in his central abdomen. Now he’s pale, diaphoretic, clutching his abdomen, and tells you the pain is “going right through to my back.” His systolic BP is 85 mmHg, heart rate 115 bpm, and he’s confused. On exam, you palpate a pulsatile, expansile mass in the epigastrium. Femoral pulses are weak but present.

**Dissect the vignette — pattern clues:**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| 72-year-old male smoker | Classic demographic: age ≥65, male, smoking history |
| Sudden, severe tearing abdominal pain radiating to back | Typical AAA rupture pain — aortic wall stretching then tearing |
| Hypotension + tachycardia + confusion | Hemorrhagic shock due to retroperitoneal or intraperitoneal bleeding |
| Pulsatile, expansile mass on palpation | Aneurysm >5.5 cm (or larger) — highly specific for AAA |
| Weak femoral pulses | Hypoperfusion from shock; rarely due to dissection extending into iliacs |
| Lifting triggered event | Valsalva-like maneuver increases intra-abdominal pressure, precipitating rupture |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **The “syncope” variant:** Elderly male collapses while shopping, no pain reported (or minimal). You find hypotension and a pulsatile mass — the rupture was contained retroperitoneally, causing vagal response +/- tamponade.
2. **The “renal colic” mimic:** Pain radiates to flank/groin, hematuria from ureteric compression, but no fever or costovertebral angle tenderness. Ultrasound shows AAA with thrombus.
3. **The “sepsis” imposter:** Patient presents with fever, hypotension, and abdominal pain. You think diverticulitis or cholangitis. But the lactate is high, abdomen distended, and US shows AAA with free fluid — infected (mycotic) AAA rupture.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Definition:**  
A rupture of the abdominal aorta through all three layers of the vessel wall (intima, media, adventitia) in a segment that has become aneurysmal (diameter ≥3.0 cm, but typically >5.5 cm for rupture risk). The tear may be contained by the retroperitoneum (contained rupture) or free into the peritoneal cavity (free rupture — catastrophic).

**Why it matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** AAA affects ~5% of men >65 years; rupture accounts for 1–2% of all deaths in this age group.  
- **Stakes:** Pre-hospital mortality for free rupture is >50%; in-hospital mortality remains 30–50% even with emergency surgery.  
- **Common pitfall:** The diagnosis is missed in 30% of cases because the presentation mimics other causes of shock or acute abdomen (e.g., pancreatitis, MI, perforated ulcer). A missed rupture is a preventable death.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of development:**  
The aorta silently weakens over years. Atherosclerosis, hypertension, smoking, and genetic factors (e.g., connective tissue disorders) cause degradation of elastin and collagen in the media. The wall thins, and the artery expands like a balloon. According to **Laplace’s Law** (wall tension = pressure × radius), as the aneurysm grows, wall stress increases exponentially. At a critical diameter (often >5.5 cm), the wall can no longer withstand the pressure — a tear occurs.

**Why each key sign happens:**

| Sign | Pathophysiologic mechanism |
|------|---------------------------|
| Sudden, tearing pain | Acute stretching of the aortic adventitia (rich in pain fibers) +/- rupture into the retroperitoneal space, which irritates nerves (including lumbar plexus — back pain) |
| Hypotension & tachycardia | Volume loss (up to 2L blood can pool in the retroperitoneum) causing hypovolemic shock. In free rupture, blood enters the peritoneal cavity — rapid decompensation |
| Pulsatile mass | The aneurysm itself is palpable. In rupture, the mass may be less distinct because of hematoma, but still expansile |
| Absent/diminished femoral pulses | Shock reduces pressure distally; if the dissection flap extends into the iliac arteries, flow obstruction worsens |
| Confusion / altered GCS | Cerebral hypoperfusion from profound hypotension |

**Connection to pattern recognition:** The classic triad (pain, shock, pulsatile mass) is present in only 30–50% of cases — but if you see any two, think rupture. The contained rupture variant may present without shock initially because the retroperitoneum tamponades the bleed. That patient can quickly decompensate when tamponade is lost (e.g., during Valsalva, movement, or aggressive fluid resuscitation).

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
**By category — with stem clues that point to each cause:**

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Degenerative (most common)** | Atherosclerosis | Elderly male smoker with hypertension; calcified aorta on X-ray |
| **Genetic / connective tissue** | Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos type IV | Young patient (<50), tall stature, arachnodactyly, lens dislocation, or easy bruising; family history of aortic dissection |
| **Infectious (mycotic AAA)** | Bacterial aortitis (Salmonella, Staphylococcus) | Fever, positive blood cultures, saccular aneurysm on CT; often in IV drug users or immunosuppressed |
| **Inflammatory (non-infectious)** | IgG4-related disease, Takayasu arteritis | Aneurysm wall is thick, periaortic fibrosis on CT; elevated ESR/CRP |
| **Traumatic** | Blunt abdominal trauma (e.g., deceleration injury) | Young patient, MVA, seatbelt sign, aortic rupture at the level of the ligamentum arteriosum (more often thoracic, but can involve abdominal aorta) |
| **Post-procedural** | Anastomotic aneurysm after previous AAA repair | History of AAA graft; new back pain or pulsatile mass at graft site |

**Highlight:** The most common cause is **atherosclerosis** (>90% of all AAAs). The trigger for rupture is often an acute event that raises intra-aortic pressure (e.g., lifting, straining, or hypertensive crisis).

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
There is no validated clinical scoring system for AAA rupture (unlike PE or ACS). Diagnosis relies on **clinical suspicion + bedside imaging**. The key threshold: an AAA diameter ≥5.5 cm in men (≥5.0 cm in women) is considered at high risk for rupture in the next year. Rupture can occur at smaller sizes, especially in women or patients with rapid expansion (>1 cm/year).

**Tests to order (sequence matters):**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Bedside ultrasound (FAST)** | Dilated aorta ≥3 cm; free fluid in retroperitoneum or peritoneum | Rule in/out AAA rapidly in unstable patient; no delay, no radiation | **Positive = AAA + free fluid** = rupture until proven otherwise. A size >5.5 cm with shock is pathognomonic. |
| **CT angiogram (CT-A)** | Exact size, extent of rupture, retroperitoneal hematoma, involvement of renal/visceral arteries | Gold standard, especially if patient is stable (SBP >90); guides surgical approach | **Single finding that confirms:** Contrast extravasation outside the aortic lumen. **Ruled out:** Normal-caliber aorta with no extravasation |
| **Chest X-ray** | May show calcified aortic outline; sometimes a widened mediastinum if rupture extends into thorax | Limited role — can be falsely normal; not recommended if high suspicion | **Unreliable.** Don't let a negative CXR delay further workup |
| **ECG** | May show signs of inferior MI (pain can mimic) | Exclude MI as alternative cause of shock/chest pain | ST elevation in II, III, aVF → consider inferior STEMI. But remember: AAA rupture can also happen in a patient with MI (coincidence or hypoperfusion causing demand ischemia) |
| **Bloods** | CBC, cross-match (type & screen), lactate, coagulation, renal function | Prepare for massive transfusion; assess shock severity | **Lactate >4 mmol/L** = severe hypoperfusion, high mortality. Hemoglobin may be initially normal (hemoconcentration) but drops with resuscitation |

**Pattern recognition — what single finding confirms / rules out:**
- **Confirms:** CT showing contrast extravasation outside the aorta. Or bedside US showing AAA + free fluid in a shocked patient.
- **Ruled out:** CT or US showing a normal caliber aorta (<3.0 cm) with no fluid. (But beware: a small AAA can rupture in women or connective tissue disorders — clinical judgment still matters.)

**What NOT to do:**  
Do not send an unstable patient to CT. The only imaging they need is a FAST scan in the resuscitation bay. Time to the OR is life.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) — the “Golden Hour” rhythm:**

| Action | Why | Details |
|--------|-----|---------|
| Call vascular surgeon & OR team | Surgery is the only definitive treatment; mortality increases ~1% per minute of delay | “I have a 72-year-old male with ruptured AAA, BP 85, in trauma bay. Need emergency EVAR or open repair.” |
| Large-bore IV access (two 14G) | Rapid volume loss; need to give blood and products | Antecubital or femoral (but avoid femoral on side of aneurysm if possible) |
| **Permissive hypotension** | Aggressive fluid raises BP → increases wall stress → worsens bleeding. Target SBP 70–90 mmHg (or mentation/peripheral pulse) | Allow SBP as low as 70 mmHg if patient is talking (cerebral perfusion). Use small boluses of 250 mL crystalloid or blood if needed. |
| Cross-match 6–10 units PRBC, FFP, platelets | Massive transfusion likely; use 1:1:1 ratio | Activate massive transfusion protocol early |
| Blood gas & lactate | Assess shock severity and base deficit | Base deficit > -6 or lactate >4 predicts poor outcome |
| **Avoid: Vasopressors** unless permissive hypotension fails to maintain SBP >70 | They increase afterload and wall tension, worsening rupture | Use low-dose norepinephrine (5–10 mcg/min) only if SBP <70 despite volume |

**Ward (24–72h) — post-operative care (if the patient survives to repair):**

| Action | Why | Details |
|--------|-----|---------|
| ICU admission | Hemodynamic monitoring, ventilation, renal support | Monitor for bleeding, graft leak, compartment syndrome |
| Optimize blood pressure | Prevent graft rupture or mesenteric ischemia | Target SBP <140, MAP >65. Use labetalol (IV 20 mg then infusion) or nicardipine (IV 5 mg/h) |
| Monitor compartment pressures | Abdominal compartment syndrome (intra-abdominal HTN) can occur from retroperitoneal hematoma | Bladder pressure >20 mmHg with organ dysfunction → surgical decompression |
| Antibiotics | If graft placed, infection risk | Cefazolin 2g IV q8h for 24h (per local protocol) |

**Long-term (after discharge):**

| Action | Why | Details |
|--------|-----|---------|
| Surveillance imaging | Graft patency, endoleak (after EVAR), or new aneurysm | CT at 1, 6, 12 months, then yearly |
| Cardiovascular risk reduction | AAA is a marker for atherosclerosis; prevent MI/stroke | Statin (e.g., atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily), antiplatelet (aspirin 75 mg PO daily), smoking cessation |
| Blood pressure control | Reduce stress on graft and native aorta | Target <130/80 mmHg; use ACEi or ARB (e.g., ramipril 5–10 mg PO daily) |

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

| Pitfall | Why it’s wrong |
|---------|----------------|
| Aggressive fluid resuscitation | Raises BP → increases wall tension → accelerates bleed. “Permissive hypotension” is key. |
| Delay surgery for CT | If patient is unstable, CT wastes time. Use FAST only. |
| Using a femoral central line | Can puncture the aneurysm or iliac artery; use EJ or internal jugular if needed. |
| Giving vasopressors first | Increases afterload and wall stress. Volume is the primary deficit. |
| Stopping after one negative US | Negative US for AAA? Still consider other causes of shock (e.g., dissection, PE). If suspicion high, do CT. |

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
The classic differentials for sudden abdominal pain with shock:

| Feature | Ruptured AAA | Acute Pancreatitis | Perforated Peptic Ulcer | Inferior STEMI | Renal Colic |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Pain location** | Central abdomen, radiating to back | Epigastric, radiates to back (band-like) | Epigastric, can be diffuse | Central chest, may be epigastric; radiation to jaw/arm | Flank to groin |
| **Pain onset** | Sudden, tearing | Gradual (over hours) | Sudden, “knife-like” | Sudden or crescendo | Sudden, colicky |
| **Associated signs** | Hypotension, pulsatile mass, weak femoral pulses | Nausea/vomiting, fever, periumbilical ecchymosis (Cullen’s) | Board-like rigidity, rebound, absent bowel sounds | Diaphoresis, nausea, dyspnea, ECG ST elevation | Nausea, hematuria, costovertebral angle tenderness |
| **ECG** | Normal (or nonspecific) | Normal | Normal | ST elevation II, III, aVF | Normal |
| **Labs** | Lactate high, Hgb may be normal initially | Amylase/lipase >3x normal | Normal amylase, possibly elevated WBC | Troponin elevated | Hematuria, normal amylase/lipase |
| **Imaging** | US: AAA + free fluid; CT: contrast extravasation | CT: pancreatic edema, necrosis | CT: free air, extravasation of oral contrast | Echo: wall motion abnormality; no aortic dilation | CT: ureteral stone, hydronephrosis |
| **Key differentiator** | Pulsatile mass + shock + AAA on US | Lipase >3x normal | Free air under diaphragm on upright CXR | ECG diagnostic | Flank pain + hematuria + stone on CT |

**Mimics of the “syncope only” variant:**
- Pulmonary embolism (obtain D-dimer/CTPA)
- Aortic dissection (chest pain, pulse deficit, mediastinal widening on CXR)
- Severe gastrointestinal bleed (melena, hematemesis, no pulsatile mass)

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complications of missed or delayed diagnosis:**

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Free rupture into peritoneum** | Sudden severe hypotension, distended abdomen, no palpable mass (blood pools freely) | Loss of tamponade; patient goes from stable to PEA arrest in minutes | Immediate OR. If in cardiac arrest, consider ED thoracotomy and aortic cross-clamp (only if experienced team) |
| **Contained rupture (retroperitoneal hematoma)** | Back pain, flank ecchymosis (Grey Turner sign), gradual hypotension over hours | Pulsatile mass still felt; BP may respond to fluids initially | Emergency CT then repair. Do not assume stability — can decompensate any moment |
| **Aortoenteric fistula** | Hematemesis or melena + AAA (presenting as upper GI bleed in patient with known AAA) | History of AAA repair or untreated large AAA; “sentinel bleed” before massive hemorrhage | Urgent endoscopy (may see bleeding in third portion of duodenum). Surgery or endovascular stent graft |
| **Renal failure** | Anuria, rising creatinine | Result of hypotension, or renal artery involvement | Renal replacement therapy if needed; aim for renal artery bypass during repair |
| **Spinal cord ischemia** | Sudden paraplegia, loss of bladder/bowel control | Aneurysm thrombus embolizes to spinal arteries (artery of Adamkiewicz) | Immediate revascularization; consider CSF drainage. Prognosis poor |
| **Leg ischemia** | Cold, pulseless, painful leg | Embolism from aneurysm thrombus or dissection into iliac artery | Embolectomy or bypass |

**Late-presentation patterns (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”):**
- **Grey Turner sign** → retroperitoneal hematoma tracking to flank. Always think AAA rupture.
- **Cullen sign** → periumbilical ecchymosis from intra-abdominal bleeding (also pancreatitis).
- **Syncope + pulsatile mass** → ruptured AAA until proven otherwise.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **“RUPTURE”** – presentation clues:  
   R = Radiating back pain  
   U = Unstable (hypotension, shock)  
   P = Pulsatile mass  
   T = Tearing sensation  
   U = Urgent OR  
   R = Risk factors (smoker, male, elderly)  
   E = Echo (US) fast

2. **“AAA – Age, Atherosclerosis, Aneurysm”**  
   Age >65 + Atherosclerosis + Abdominal pain + Absent femoral pulses = Aortic Aneurysm ruptured.

3. **“Don’t Bolus, Don’t Vaso – Just Cross and Go”**  
   For management: Permissive hypotension (no bolus), no vasopressors if possible, cross-match blood, go to OR.

**High-yield clinical pearls (pattern recognition):**

1. **Think AAA in any elderly male with unexplained shock** – even without pain. The “syncope variant” is classic.
2. **Bedside ultrasound is your best friend** – FAST scan includes aortic view. Learn it. A 5-second sweep can save a life.
3. **A normal CXR does not rule out AAA rupture** – many have no mediastinal widening.
4. **Hemoglobin can be normal initially** in a contained rupture due to hemoconcentration. Do not be reassured – lactate and shock are the real indicators.
5. **If you feel a pulsatile mass in a hypotensive patient, do not press hard** – you may cause free rupture. Gentle palpation only.
6. **The patient who “stabilizes” after 2L fluids is at risk** – that fluid will worsen bleeding. Target SBP 70–90, not >100.
7. **In women, AAA rupture can occur at smaller sizes** (<5.0 cm). Don’t dismiss small aneurysms in females with shock.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- **“The patient has a history of diverticulitis, so this must be diverticulitis.”** Confirmation bias kills. Always scan for AAA in elderly hypotensive patients.
- **“Let’s wait for labs.”** No – the only lab that matters is “OR now.”
- **“We need a CT to plan surgery.”** Yes for stable patients, but if BP <90, they go to OR, not CT.
- **“Let’s give a fluid bolus and see if they improve.”** You’ll see them deteriorate.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call for help immediately (do not pass GO):**

| Situation | Action | Who to call | Threshold |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Suspected AAA rupture (any single clue) | Stop everything. Start permissive hypotension, 2 large-bore IVs, cross-match | **Vascular surgeon** + **ICU outreach/consult** | Clinical suspicion alone – do not wait for imaging |
| Hypotension unresponsive to minimal fluids (SBP <70 after 500 mL) | Activate massive transfusion protocol, prepare for OR | **Crash team** if near-arrest | NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4 |
| GCS <13 or falling | Protect airway, call anesthesia | **Anaesthesia + ICU** | GCS <9 → intubate |
| Cardiac arrest (PEA) | If suspected AAA rupture, consider ED thoracotomy (only if surgeon present) | **Cardiothoracic/vascular + crash team** | Pulseless electrical activity (PEA) |
| Post-operative: new severe back pain, hypotension, or falling Hb | Suspect graft leak or endoleak after repair | **Vascular surgeon** | Hb drop >2 g/dL in 4 hours |
| Abdominal compartment syndrome (bladder pressure >20 mmHg + oliguria) | Request surgical decompression | **General/Vascular surgeon** | Bladder pressure >20 with organ failure |

**Remember:** The single most important escalation is the minute you suspect AAA rupture. The phone call to the vascular surgeon should happen *before* the CT scan, before the blood results, before the IV fluids. Time is tissue.

# Ruptured Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm

**One-liner:** A sudden, catastrophic breach of a weakened aortic wall in elderly smokers/males—presenting with shock, abdominal/back pain, and a pulsatile mass; time-to-OR defines survival.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 72-year-old male smoker with known hypertension arrives via ambulance. He was lifting a heavy box at home when he felt a sudden “tearing” sensation in his central abdomen. Now he’s pale, diaphoretic, clutching his abdomen, and tells you the pain is “going right through to my back.” His systolic BP is 85 mmHg, heart rate 115 bpm, and he’s confused. On exam, you palpate a pulsatile, expansile mass in the epigastrium. Femoral pulses are weak but present.

**Dissect the vignette — pattern clues:**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| 72-year-old male smoker | Classic demographic: age ≥65, male, smoking history |
| Sudden, severe tearing abdominal pain radiating to back | Typical AAA rupture pain — aortic wall stretching then tearing |
| Hypotension + tachycardia + confusion | Hemorrhagic shock due to retroperitoneal or intraperitoneal bleeding |
| Pulsatile, expansile mass on palpation | Aneurysm >5.5 cm (or larger) — highly specific for AAA |
| Weak femoral pulses | Hypoperfusion from shock; rarely due to dissection extending into iliacs |
| Lifting triggered event | Valsalva-like maneuver increases intra-abdominal pressure, precipitating rupture |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **The “syncope” variant:** Elderly male collapses while shopping, no pain reported (or minimal). You find hypotension and a pulsatile mass — the rupture was contained retroperitoneally, causing vagal response +/- tamponade.
2. **The “renal colic” mimic:** Pain radiates to flank/groin, hematuria from ureteric compression, but no fever or costovertebral angle tenderness. Ultrasound shows AAA with thrombus.
3. **The “sepsis” imposter:** Patient presents with fever, hypotension, and abdominal pain. You think diverticulitis or cholangitis. But the lactate is high, abdomen distended, and US shows AAA with free fluid — infected (mycotic) AAA rupture.

| Sign | Pathophysiologic mechanism |
|------|---------------------------|

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

**By category — with stem clues that point to each cause:**

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|

| **Inflammatory (non-infectious)** | IgG4-related disease, Takayasu arteritis | Aneurysm wall is thick, periaortic fibrosis on CT; elevated ESR/CRP |

- **Confirms:** CT showing contrast extravasation outside the aorta. Or bedside US showing AAA + free fluid in a shocked patient.

| Surveillance imaging | Graft patency, endoleak (after EVAR), or new aneurysm | CT at 1, 6, 12 months, then yearly |
| Cardiovascular risk reduction | AAA is a marker for atherosclerosis; prevent MI/stroke | Statin (e.g., atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily), antiplatelet (aspirin 75 mg PO daily), smoking cessation |
| Blood pressure control | Reduce stress on graft and native aorta | Target <130/80 mmHg; use ACEi or ARB (e.g., ramipril 5–10 mg PO daily) |

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

| Pitfall | Why it’s wrong |
|---------|----------------|
| Aggressive fluid resuscitation | Raises BP → increases wall tension → accelerates bleed. “Permissive hypotension” is key. |
| Delay surgery for CT | If patient is unstable, CT wastes time. Use FAST only. |
| Using a femoral central line | Can puncture the aneurysm or iliac artery; use EJ or internal jugular if needed. |
| Giving vasopressors first | Increases afterload and wall stress. Volume is the primary deficit. |
| Stopping after one negative US | Negative US for AAA? Still consider other causes of shock (e.g., dissection, PE). If suspicion high, do CT. |

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

The classic differentials for sudden abdominal pain with shock:

| Feature | Ruptured AAA | Acute Pancreatitis | Perforated Peptic Ulcer | Inferior STEMI | Renal Colic |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Pain location** | Central abdomen, radiating to back | Epigastric, radiates to back (band-like) | Epigastric, can be diffuse | Central chest, may be epigastric; radiation to jaw/arm | Flank to groin |
| **Pain onset** | Sudden, tearing | Gradual (over hours) | Sudden, “knife-like” | Sudden or crescendo | Sudden, colicky |
| **Associated signs** | Hypotension, pulsatile mass, weak femoral pulses | Nausea/vomiting, fever, periumbilical ecchymosis (Cullen’s) | Board-like rigidity, rebound, absent bowel sounds | Diaphoresis, nausea, dyspnea, ECG ST elevation | Nausea, hematuria, costovertebral angle tenderness |
| **ECG** | Normal (or nonspecific) | Normal | Normal | ST elevation II, III, aVF | Normal |
| **Labs** | Lactate high, Hgb may be normal initially | Amylase/lipase >3x normal | Normal amylase, possibly elevated WBC | Troponin elevated | Hematuria, normal amylase/lipase |
| **Imaging** | US: AAA + free fluid; CT: contrast extravasation | CT: pancreatic edema, necrosis | CT: free air, extravasation of oral contrast | Echo: wall motion abnormality; no aortic dilation | CT: ureteral stone, hydronephrosis |
| **Key differentiator** | Pulsatile mass + shock + AAA on US | Lipase >3x normal | Free air under diaphragm on upright CXR | ECG diagnostic | Flank pain + hematuria + stone on CT |

**Mimics of the “syncope only” variant:**
- Pulmonary embolism (obtain D-dimer/CTPA)
- Aortic dissection (chest pain, pulse deficit, mediastinal widening on CXR)
- Severe gastrointestinal bleed (melena, hematemesis, no pulsatile mass)

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complications of missed or delayed diagnosis:**

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Free rupture into peritoneum** | Sudden severe hypotension, distended abdomen, no palpable mass (blood pools freely) | Loss of tamponade; patient goes from stable to PEA arrest in minutes | Immediate OR. If in cardiac arrest, consider ED thoracotomy and aortic cross-clamp (only if experienced team) |
| **Contained rupture (retroperitoneal hematoma)** | Back pain, flank ecchymosis (Grey Turner sign), gradual hypotension over hours | Pulsatile mass still felt; BP may respond to fluids initially | Emergency CT then repair. Do not assume stability — can decompensate any moment |
| **Aortoenteric fistula** | Hematemesis or melena + AAA (presenting as upper GI bleed in patient with known AAA) | History of AAA repair or untreated large AAA; “sentinel bleed” before massive hemorrhage | Urgent endoscopy (may see bleeding in third portion of duodenum). Surgery or endovascular stent graft |
| **Renal failure** | Anuria, rising creatinine | Result of hypotension, or renal artery involvement | Renal replacement therapy if needed; aim for renal artery bypass during repair |
| **Spinal cord ischemia** | Sudden paraplegia, loss of bladder/bowel control | Aneurysm thrombus embolizes to spinal arteries (artery of Adamkiewicz) | Immediate revascularization; consider CSF drainage. Prognosis poor |
| **Leg ischemia** | Cold, pulseless, painful leg | Embolism from aneurysm thrombus or dissection into iliac artery | Embolectomy or bypass |

**Late-presentation patterns (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”):**
- **Grey Turner sign** → retroperitoneal hematoma tracking to flank. Always think AAA rupture.
- **Cullen sign** → periumbilical ecchymosis from intra-abdominal bleeding (also pancreatitis).
- **Syncope + pulsatile mass** → ruptured AAA until proven otherwise.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **“RUPTURE”** – presentation clues:  
   R = Radiating back pain  
   U = Unstable (hypotension, shock)  
   P = Pulsatile mass  
   T = Tearing sensation  
   U = Urgent OR  
   R = Risk factors (smoker, male, elderly)  
   E = Echo (US) fast

2. **“AAA – Age, Atherosclerosis, Aneurysm”**  
   Age >65 + Atherosclerosis + Abdominal pain + Absent femoral pulses = Aortic Aneurysm ruptured.

3. **“Don’t Bolus, Don’t Vaso – Just Cross and Go”**  
   For management: Permissive hypotension (no bolus), no vasopressors if possible, cross-match blood, go to OR.

**High-yield clinical pearls (pattern recognition):**

1. **Think AAA in any elderly male with unexplained shock** – even without pain. The “syncope variant” is classic.
2. **Bedside ultrasound is your best friend** – FAST scan includes aortic view. Learn it. A 5-second sweep can save a life.
3. **A normal CXR does not rule out AAA rupture** – many have no mediastinal widening.
4. **Hemoglobin can be normal initially** in a contained rupture due to hemoconcentration. Do not be reassured – lactate and shock are the real indicators.
5. **If you feel a pulsatile mass in a hypotensive patient, do not press hard** – you may cause free rupture. Gentle palpation only.
6. **The patient who “stabilizes” after 2L fluids is at risk** – that fluid will worsen bleeding. Target SBP 70–90, not >100.
7. **In women, AAA rupture can occur at smaller sizes** (<5.0 cm). Don’t dismiss small aneurysms in females with shock.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- **“The patient has a history of diverticulitis, so this must be diverticulitis.”** Confirmation bias kills. Always scan for AAA in elderly hypotensive patients.
- **“Let’s wait for labs.”** No – the only lab that matters is “OR now.”
- **“We need a CT to plan surgery.”** Yes for stable patients, but if BP <90, they go to OR, not CT.
- **“Let’s give a fluid bolus and see if they improve.”** You’ll see them deteriorate.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call for help immediately (do not pass GO):**

| Situation | Action | Who to call | Threshold |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Suspected AAA rupture (any single clue) | Stop everything. Start permissive hypotension, 2 large-bore IVs, cross-match | **Vascular surgeon** + **ICU outreach/consult** | Clinical suspicion alone – do not wait for imaging |
| Hypotension unresponsive to minimal fluids (SBP <70 after 500 mL) | Activate massive transfusion protocol, prepare for OR | **Crash team** if near-arrest | NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4 |
| GCS <13 or falling | Protect airway, call anesthesia | **Anaesthesia + ICU** | GCS <9 → intubate |
| Cardiac arrest (PEA) | If suspected AAA rupture, consider ED thoracotomy (only if surgeon present) | **Cardiothoracic/vascular + crash team** | Pulseless electrical activity (PEA) |
| Post-operative: new severe back pain, hypotension, or falling Hb | Suspect graft leak or endoleak after repair | **Vascular surgeon** | Hb drop >2 g/dL in 4 hours |
| Abdominal compartment syndrome (bladder pressure >20 mmHg + oliguria) | Request surgical decompression | **General/Vascular surgeon** | Bladder pressure >20 with organ failure |

**Remember:** The single most important escalation is the minute you suspect AAA rupture. The phone call to the vascular surgeon should happen *before* the CT scan, before the blood results, before the IV fluids. Time is tissue.
